summary: Gruber responds to Larry Page's I/O statement that he finds 'us versus some other company' narratives uninteresting and that Google should focus on building things that don't exist. Gruber systematically dismantles this claim by listing Google's history of entering existing markets: Android after iPhone, Google Plus after Facebook, Gmail after webmail, Google Search after earlier search engines, and Google Maps after MapQuest. He argues that Google is fundamentally a hyper-competitive company that succeeds by making better versions of existing products, not by inventing new categories. While he finds nothing wrong with this strategy, he calls out the disconnect between Page's rhetoric and Google's actual behavior.
key_points: Larry Page's claim that Google builds 'great things that don't exist' is contradicted by Google's own product history
Google's biggest successes — Search, Android, Gmail, Maps, Google Plus — were all entries into existing markets
Google's strategy is making dramatically better versions of existing products, not creating new categories
Google's RSS reader history shows they can enter a market, destroy competitors, then abandon it
There's nothing wrong with being hyper-competitive, but pretending otherwise is disingenuous
money_quotes: Google fans seem to eat this kumbaya stuff up, to really believe it.
Google is the company that built Android after the iPhone, Google Plus after Facebook, and now a subscription music service after Spotify.
They entered the RSS reader market, wiped it out, and are now just walking away from it.
Google search wasn't something new; it was something better. Way, way, way better, but still.
Google is a hyper-competitive company, and they repeatedly enter markets that already exist and crush competitors.
There's nothing stupid about seeing Google being pitted 'versus' other companies. They want everything; their ambition is boundless.
key_insight: Google's self-image as a company that builds things that don't exist is flatly contradicted by its actual history of entering existing markets and crushing incumbents.
